perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information means all confidential information (however recorded, preserved or disclosed) disclosed by a party or its Representatives to the other party a nd that party's Representatives including but not limited to: (a) The fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations; (b) The existence and terms of this Agreement; (c) Any information that would be regarded as confidential by a reasonable business person relating to: (i) The business, affairs, custo mers, clients, SAMPLE2 suppliers, plans , inte ntions, or market opportunities of the Disclosing Party; and (ii) The operations, processes, product information, know -how, designs, trade secrets or s oftware of the Disclosing Party; and (d) Any information or analysis derived from Confidential Information; but not including any information that: (e) Is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of its disclosure by the Recipient or its Representa tives in breach of this Agreement or of any other undertaking of confidentiality addressed to the party to whom the information relates (except that any compilation of otherwise public information in a form not publicly known shall nevertheless be treated as Confidential Information); or (f) Was available to the Recipient on a non - confidential basis prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; or (g) Was lawfully in the possession of the Recipient before the information was disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party; or (h) The parties agree in writing is not confidential or may be disclosed .
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information means all confidential information (however recorded, preserved or disclosed) disclosed by a party or its Representatives to the other party and that party's Representatives including but not limited to: (a) The fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations; (b) The existence and terms of this Agreement; (c) Any information that would be regarded as confidential by a reasonable business person relating to: (i) The business, affairs, custo mers, clients, SAMPLE2 suppliers, plans , inte ntions, or market opportunities of the Disclosing Party; and (ii) The operations, processes, product information, know -how, designs, trade secrets or s oftware of the Disclosing Party; and (d) Any information or analysis derived from Confidential Information; but not including any information that: (e) Is or becomes generally available to the public; or (f) Was available to the Recipient on a non - confidential basis prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; or (g) Was lawfully in the possession of the Recipient before the information was disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party; or (h) The parties agree in writing is not confidential or may be disclosed .
perturbation.explanation: This change creates an in-text contradiction by removing the clause 'other than as a result of its disclosure by the Recipient or its Representa tives in breach of this Agreement or of any other undertaking of confidentiality addressed to the party to whom the information relates (except that any compilation of otherwise public information in a form not publicly known shall nevertheless be treated as Confidential Information)' from the definition of 'Confidential Information'. Now information disclosed due to a breach of the agreement would no longer be confidential. The omission contradicts the obligations outlined in Section 2, which states that the Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential. If information that becomes public due to a breach is no longer considered Confidential Information, it undermines the fundamental purpose of the NDA.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, shall: (a) Warrants and undertake that it is solely responsible for ensuring that the Personal Data is processed by it in accordance with the Data Protection Act 1998 (“the Act”) from the date that it is received from the Disclosing Party; (b) Not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose. If the Recipient proposes to use or process the Personal Data for a purpose other than the Purpose, the Recipient shall ensure that, prior to using or processing the Personal Data in such manner, it will ensure that such use or process is in compliance with the Act; (c) Not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement; and (d) Not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except as strictly necessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Disclosing Party).
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes a clause specifying that information disclosed due to a breach of the agreement remains confidential. This directly contradicts the contradicted text (Section 2), which mandates that the recipient must keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential. The change implies that information that becomes public due to a breach is no longer considered Confidential Information, thus undermining the obligation in Section 2 and creating a strong in-text contradiction as defined by the provided definition for 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, shall: (a) Warrants and undertake that it is solely responsible for ensuring that the Personal Data is processed by it in accordance with the Data Protection Act 1998 (“the Act”) from the date that it is received from the Disclosing Party; (b) Not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose. If the Recipient proposes to use or process the Personal Data for a purpose other than the Purpose, the Recipient shall ensure that, prior to using or processing the Personal Data in such manner, it will ensure that such use or process is in compliance with the Act; (c) Not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement; and (d) Not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except as strictly necessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Disclosing Party).
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, shall: (a) Warrants and undertake that it is solely responsible for ensuring that the Personal Data is processed by it in accordance with the Data Protection Act 1998 (“the Act”) from the date that it is received from the Disclosing Party; (b) Not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose. If the Recipient proposes to use or process the Personal Data for a purpose other than the Purpose, the Recipient shall ensure that, prior to using or processing the Personal Data in such manner, it will ensure that such use or process is in compliance with the Act; (c) Not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement; and (d) Copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information regardless of the necessity for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Disclosing Party).
perturbation.explanation: This introduces a contradiction to the confidentiality obligations of the agreement. Section 2.1 (d) originally limits the recipient's ability to copy or record the confidential information to only when strictly necessary for the stated purpose. Modifying it to allow copying or recording 'regardless of the necessity for the Purpose' directly conflicts with the first part of section 2.1 that obligates the Recipient to keep the disclosing party's confidential information confidential and only use it for the purpose. This opens up the possibility of using the information for other purposes.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, shall: (a) Warrants and undertake that it is solely responsible for ensuring that the Personal Data is processed by it in accordance with the Data Protection Act 1998 (“the Act”) from the date that it is received from the Disclosing Party; (b) Not use or exploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose. If the Recipient proposes to use or process the Personal Data for a purpose other than the Purpose, the Recipient shall ensure that, prior to using or processing the Personal Data in such manner, it will ensure that such use or process is in compliance with the Act; (c) Not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this Agreement; and (d) Copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information regardless of the necessity for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Disclosing Party).
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies section 2.1(d) to allow copying and recording of confidential information 'regardless of the necessity for the Purpose,' while the contradicted text (which is also the original section 2.1) emphasizes the Recipient's obligation to keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and only use it for the purpose. This creates a direct conflict, as the changed text permits actions that could compromise confidentiality and facilitate uses beyond the stated purpose, thereby contradicting the initial obligation to maintain confidentiality and use the information solely for the Purpose. This aligns with the definition of inconsistencies that leads to in-text contradictions when different sections of a contract provide conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: No party shall make, or permit any person to make, any public announcement concerning this Agreement, the Purpose or its prospective interest in the Purpose without the prior written consent of the other party (such consent not to be unreasonably withheld or delayed) except as required by law or any governmental or regulatory authority (including, without limitation, any relevant securities exchange) or by any court or other authority of competent jurisdiction. No party shall make use of the other party's name or any information acquired through its dealings with the other party for publicity or marketing purposes without the prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: No party shall make, or permit any person to make, any public announcement concerning this Agreement, the Purpose or its prospective interest in the Purpose except as required by law or any governmental or regulatory authority (including, without limitation, any relevant securities exchange) or by any court or other authority of competent jurisdiction. No party shall make use of the other party's name or any information acquired through its dealings with the other party for publicity or marketing purposes without the prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: This change causes a contradiction by removing the phrase '(such consent not to be unreasonably withheld or delayed)' from the section prohibiting public announcements. The original clause indicates that consent for public announcement cannot be 'unreasonably withheld'. By removing this, it grants the other party the power to withhold consent for any reason, which contradicts the standard of reasonable behavior assumed in contracts. This allows a party to prevent any and all publicity. The first phrase is contradicted by the rest of the section. Overall, this changes the nature of what is enforced in this section.
perturbation.contradicted_text: No party shall make, or permit any person to make, any public announcement concerning this Agreement, the Purpose or its prospective interest in the Purpose except as required by law or any governmental or regulatory authority (including, without limitation, any relevant securities exchange) or by any court or other authority of competent jurisdiction. No party shall make use of the other party's name or any information acquired through its dealings with the other party for publicity or marketing purposes without the prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.justification: The change removes the phrase '(such consent not to be unreasonably withheld or delayed)' from the section prohibiting public announcements, as described in the Perturbation Explanation. The original text required consent to not be 'unreasonably withheld', but the changed text allows the other party to withhold consent for any reason, which contradicts the standard of reasonable behavior assumed in contracts. The changed text directly conflicts with the original intent regarding public announcements, thus resulting in a strong in-text contradiction according to the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties and supersedes and extinguishes all previous drafts, agreements, arrangements and understandings between them, whether written or oral, relating to its subject matter.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties regarding confidentiality and supersedes and extinguishes all previous drafts, agreements, arrangements and understandings between them, whether written or oral, relating to its subject matter.
perturbation.explanation: The original Entire Agreement clause states that the current agreement is the entirety of the understanding between the parties. By modifying it to say the agreement is only the entire agreement regarding confidentiality, the change creates a contradiction. Now, it's unclear whether any previous agreements or understandings about other aspects of the relationship (besides confidentiality) are still valid. This contradicts the purpose of the clause which is to clear out all previous arrangements, by implying there are still valid agreements about aspects that aren't confidentiality. Now, the parties could argue that certain oral agreements could still be relevant.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties regarding confidentiality and supersedes and extinguishes all previous drafts, agreements, arrangements and understandings between them, whether written or oral, relating to its subject matter.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the agreement is the entire agreement between the parties regarding all subject matters, superseding all previous agreements. The changed text narrows this scope to only confidentiality, implying that previous agreements on other subjects might still be valid. This directly contradicts the purpose of an entire agreement clause, which is to extinguish all previous arrangements. Since the contradicted text and the changed text are the same, there is a strong contradiction.
